speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Carl Olson tells many anecdotes about local people and events of Troy's past. He speaks for the importance of environment in people's lives and for government that works for the people. He questions the seriousness of the Great Depression in Latah County. He explains the causes of smut fires in the harvest fields and also how he knew there'd be water where the city of Troy recently drilled a well. Behind the bobsled he charges a dollar and a half a piece. We had a couple of suitcases, you know, we put that on the sled.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was to get out of the Mizpah mine?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, or just that much, I guess, to carry them satchels out. It was no box on it, you know, the bunks only. He hauled lumber on, you know, lumber. There was no place to sit. We walked after the sled, three of us. We sure got a kick out of that when he charged us a dollar and a half a piece to walk after the sled.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far did he take you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, from Hoodoo to Harvard, I don't know, maybe about six, seven miles, I guess.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that story about working the Mizpah Mine? I mean, what did you do there?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, what we did, we just had a court rail and drilled holes in the feet we could find in the men's rails. But they had a tunnel before these people. Jones used to be the head of the hard -line slumber company. He used to be a guy, you know. T .P. Jones. Yeah, T .P. Jones. He was along with us in that oil setup in Montana too, T .P. Jones. We worked for him quite a bit.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But people there went busted up in Montana. He started a company of his own then afterwards.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He was in with the regional company, Troy Sweetgrass, what's the name of that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Then when Jones went in, they changed it to Potlach Oil and Refining Company. And then when they went pretty well broke, T .P. had a company of his own there. He had so much land, he had about 10 ,000 acres at least. Then they drilled a deep test, about 3 ,000 -4 ,000 feet down, I guess, and that broke them, finished them up. It wasn't that he was there much anyway, it was some small whales and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he quit Potlach to go into this Montana?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, he was still with them then, yet alright, but he quit them after a while. He made a change then, Potlach, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So he was pretty old then anyway, he reached time for him to retire anyhow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did you work the Mizpah for him? How long did you work the Mizpah for him?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, not very long, just one season in the fall of 1919. That's when they struck that rich spot there in Mizpah, you know, showing you the picture of the stuff everybody loved by them bags. Got a kind of excitement in the mountain then, you know, and any one of them drilled down there to see if he had anything. But that was the hearing only about that thick, you know, laying this way. Maybe a couple of feet was all. Yeah, and they got two or three corals out of it too, and it laid on the top of the ground practically. But the experts said, the way that happened, there's never been no vein there, never. The mountain was high, see, and it kept seeping down. And then when it got down to the water level, it stopped there and there's where they deposited that copper water, you see. That's how that happened. There was no vein, you see. It just happened to be that mountain had been maybe twice as high as it is now one time, see. And that's why we didn't find any veins up there, just small stringers running through, as big as your arm, any. Rich in copper, see. And they didn't fall in them, and they didn't get no better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was because it had all just settled down?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, that's the way the whole mountain has been. That's what they claim. And all those small veins, you know, when the water went through, it leached it and put it in there. And when it got through the water level, it wouldn't go no deeper, so it built up that copper basin in there, see. Yep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you and your brother the only two that worked the mine?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, I and Bob. And them had a better way to name some. Nelson, he was Cook, we had Cook, and we helped outside, too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But he had other people working there before our time. They sunk a shaft, you know, see. That's slow work, you know. That's why he wanted to have it core -railed up and see. But we didn't find anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much other mining going on in Hoodoos then when you were up there?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It wasn't very much then. It's all, Peter, see, Hoodoo, they had a lot of gold there, you know, early days. Not a lot of it, but it's enough to mine, you know. So the whole mountain was full of miners there long time. That's before my time for me. And then they come up there and dredged it afterwards. That is an awful long goal, you know, since the dredge. On the river. So it was gold there, you know, right, early days. And that's what brought the miners down this way, you know. They were looking for gold all over, you see. They struck that big gold in California in 49, you know. And 60 over here in Pierce Country, 1960. They brought people in there, you know. So they found every time any man really wants to be found, they found it, you know. But they were looking for gold. That's how they happened to find down there. That's Camp Canyon, you know. Down with Troy here, we worked there. And there they incorporate on the top there is flat and they can go down. So this meant people stand quite a bit down in Camp Canyon, too. And down on the one we had, but that vein went down all right. But it was only about that wide. For the rich, though.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So it was everything here in the early days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, not many families had mines, did they?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I mean, it was pretty rare that family would have a mine right by the homestead like you did. Well, there was no family. There was Cox that found it the first time. He was a single man, you know. He had a cabin there. No stove in it or nothing. He had a little fireplace and he baked his bread on the hot rocks, you know. And the cabin, no window in it. He could do it all by himself. Then he'd go out in the harvest or something, you know, and get a few dollars. And then he'd come back in the winter and mine there, you see. That's the way he worked.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He asked him, have you hit anything yet?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, he said, I'll get daylight for the zone that keeps on because you're on a ridge like this. And he started here and he went right through the ridge.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And now... But the farms and the timber have made this country. There was no mineral here. Really, they got the clay though, but that came in later, about 1914, I think, 12 or 14. Started making fire brick here. A lot of clay here. I worked with the government. When the Second World War was on, they were afraid they would run out of aluminum. So it was aluminum in the clay, you know. So we drilled the whole spot between here and there, you know. Hand -drilled, the augers went down 400 feet or more once in a while. Then we sent that in and had it analyzed since we had much aluminum was in it. It was quite a bit, you know, but then the war stopped and then the conduct come in and took up the leases afterwards. And this was going to start. And it dug quite a hole up in there on the farm of the Bremen's. But they gave it up too. Because it's another kind of clay that runs around 40 percent aluminum, I believe. That's we reached. This run about 20 up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did your mine get the name Copper King?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Copper Chief. Well, it was an old man. And Ferrara put the name on. He was a mining engineer, you know. And he said, I'm staking my reputation on this mine, so I'll name it for you. Copper Chief, see.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, but it didn't pan out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One of those pictures that you showed me, there was a get -together down at the mine, a whole bunch of people down there?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, it was like on a Sunday, young people get together and then they'd go down to the mine. Because you'd go in the tunnels, you know. And that was kind of a kick for them to go in the tunnels. One picture is taken in the tunnel, you know. Cox's tunnel. It's dry because that's higher up. And then you got one under, you see. And that drain, that too, so it's just the driest powder in there. And when he was hollering about these here shelters, you know, bomb shelters here a few years ago, I told the guy, we got one already down there. Whole couple hundred people, I said, we'll have to do nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How close is the mine to the homestead?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, it's only half a mile. It borders on my dad's play, homestead. But the homestead, homestead right in there, there, you see, like this. There was three -fourtys along this way, that's three -quarters of a mile, see. And this 40 here, that's where the mine was. That isn't even taken. It belongs to the government yet. Nobody could take it because there was mineral on it. And the fella next to him, he had quite a time to get his homestead clean because there was too much mineral there, you know. People put him protested, you know. So they had to have my dad and some more was up and testified and told him it wasn't enough. So he got his papers all right on the crates.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh,

speaker: Carl Olson
words: it's a possibility, it is mineral there all right. If you could go down, we're on it, you know. Because the main goes down. It wouldn't lay flat. But that takes a lot of money.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then we drove the lower tunnel that was below all these tunnels, that 300 -foot one I told you about, you know. Then we hit the... I got some more memory. This one looks like silver anyway, I guess. Cobalt. Cobalt, yeah. So we took the police up to university and then the men down there and looked all over the side hills and they said, Can't find any sign of it here at all on the top. And it was no way neither down where we hit it. It was a chunk here and there in the rocks, see. So then we was going to go in and do some mining on it. Then the whole tunnel just came in, you know. So we couldn't get in and it cost too much to open it up, too much work. So we got a hold of this diamond drill for $800, I believe we paid for it. And we drilled. The surveyors had surveyed for us so we knew exactly where to put the drill, you know. Because the people that studied mining in university, they come down through the mine, yet my brother said, and look at it and so forth. So this is the way, by God, this is the way it perfectly hit. We hit the tunnel all right. But we had to go down about 180 feet. When we got down there we hit the tunnel, see. So then we moved and made another hole, but we didn't get no cobra in the core, so. Because it was spotted, you know. You had to hit one of those spots to get some.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you boys get by when you were living at that cabin you had by the mine? Did you cook for yourself and everything and not go home very much?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, we stayed there. The picture of it was sitting by the stove there. Bob, he was the cook. And the drill sharpener, you know, you had to sharpen the rails and tamper them, you know. So we could get down. That's what he done and we done the mining.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That's the picture you like to use, I guess. No, I saw them. We done that in the winter. We didn't mine in the summer any. And there I am. Lightened the fuse, you see. You can see there hangs one. There's another one. Six of them, that's the life. Get the hell out of there.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like up in Mizpah? Was it real isolated for you up there when you were working?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, at the Northrop outfit, they had the mine then. He come up there once in a while. He didn't do no mining, you know. He was hustling with this oreo farm, you know, on the top then. He didn't do any tunneling at all. I don't think they ever done any more after that either. Well, I guess maybe they went in somewhere and tunneled alright and looked around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about T .P. Jones? Did you know him very well?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: We did. Oh yeah. He had a big car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the name of the car anyway?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The six cylinder.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a Hudson?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, I think so. We used to ride with him all the time. He'd go up to the mine, let us take us back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He was a big guy. Not too raw -boned. He wasn't fat, but he was fairly big. He got a relative in Troy here. His name was Jones too. Then he had another brother or two. Now this isn't his brother. He had a brother or two up around the world with him, but I forgot what his name was. He worked for the Potlatch too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, I wanted to start asking you some of those things that we never put down that you were telling me about the last couple of times I was here. Like one of the things was you telling me about Osterburg. Oh, Osterburg.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't I tell you that? Well, you told me part of it. We never put it down. I really thought that was funny stuff.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, you told me part of it. We never put it down. I really thought that was funny stuff. Well, oh, but catching all the fish and crossing the quake by no right. We rode back and told them a good country would come to you. They said they took 12 horses to pull the carrots out of the ground.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And here I stood and shot 40, 50 deer right here. Then he shot one behind the big bull pine, you know, big tree like this.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And he said, how can you hit him behind there?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, he said, I sighted all the trees. And then when I pulled the trigger, I yanked the gun around and the bullet went up.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He was quite a guy. And about the hills, you know, they say they come from Minnesota quite a few of them here. And he was too. You know, with level ground there. So then they rode back.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then they rode over here to Osterburg and said, that's pretty hilly out there, though, isn't it?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He bragged everything else up, you know, so they thought he was going to get the best from there. Hilly, that's nothing. They said it all leaned downhill.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, he was quite a character, all right. He had an old muscle -loader gun, you know, too. You know, the old muscle -loader, he had to load this way. And then you put the primer on. You had two or three of them. You know, he was quite a guy.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He got way up in the 90s before he died. He said he was going to live until he gets under, but he didn't quite make it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the one about the fish, you started to say?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, the fish, he said it's a creek running. There's no bridges then, you know. Of course, come here, you know, so they have to drive over the creeks, you know. So he said, when I blow where I live, he said I have to cross the creek. And my wagon is heading. And when I got across the creek, I had a half full of fish. Wheels caught and throwed up in there, you said.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He could tell it. There's a lot more to it, but I can't remember all of it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I was going to tell you about them two bachelors at home, and they were sitting together, you know. There's nothing funny about them. A lot of people done that. They built their cabin on the line, you know. So half of them built this and half of that, and they lived together there. You see, they didn't have to build many cabins. But these fellow neighbors had no to add to themselves. They wore the same kind of hat, same kind of coat, same kind of pants, same kind of a pipe, they smoked pipe, you know. And if one lit the pipe, the other one would light at the same time with the same match if it didn't burn up too fast. And they said, let's go to town today. Yes, that was all the truth. They say, let's stay home. Yeah, that's all. That's where they lived. Yes, no argument at all between the fellers. Not at all.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And they had the same clothes. If he bought a pair of pants, the other one bought one at the same time. The shoes the same way. So they found this older place because there was quite a bit of timber on it, and they bought the place out here. But he couldn't live there. He said, Magnus was the name of one. Magnus walks around the house all night, he says. We can't sleep. We have to get back. And I've seen the two because I worked on the Nora Mill and I see them come by every day in their buggy, you know. Drive up to this place and then you go back, and you drive up to that, and that's the way they kept going for three, four weeks, I think. And it was pretty loose in their mind, you know. So they finally had to buy the place back, and then they moved up there again. You know, I knew them well. I was trash -focused. And then people said, I wonder what will happen to one of them if one happened to die. The way they, you know, they lived together so tight, that was pretty one person you might say. But it didn't affect the man. It didn't affect Oren died. He went down to stay with the widow down there in Croixville. He died. They thought maybe he was a loony or something, you know. Just a few hella -sons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was one of them, did one of them get married?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No. No, they never got married.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, it was fun to watch them, you know, when they lit the pipe, you know, and they looked away, and then they'd hand the match over to the other one. And they looked for it. They were about the same size, too, so they looked pretty much the same. To look at them, they wasn't like a pair of twin stones. Yeah, that was only a couple of miles east of where you live, where the homestead was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was their name again? What was their name again?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Joe Hansen. Magnus and John.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Used to be his old army right below, they called it the Dry Creek Army. They lived right above that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people make fun of them very much?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, no, we always talked about them, though, where they got along so good, you know, and then they were so ominous, you know. So when they logged down to the mill when I was working there, the ex -l -bowman, he said, scale your own logs, so they wouldn't have to scale for you. So they scaled their own timber and everything. Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I'll tell you a story about Butcher Johnson here that I told you happened. He used to have a butcher here, he had a homestead right up there where we drilled this new well on his face. Well, a fire extinguisher man come down there. He owned a building down there over there. The one Strome was in there, I believe. The one Strome was in there. And then he slept upstairs. He was a widow then. And a fire extinguisher man come around there, and he sent me these bulbs, you know, to hang up. And when it gets so hot, they fall down and smash, you see. He got them down. So he went in to the felony in Price. He had a restaurant below. And told Price, he says to Price, I'm selling fire extinguishers, he says. I like to sell. He said, well, I don't own the building. It's in the proprietary upstairs. So he was going, if you go up there, maybe you can sell him. So this fella, he said he lives in room four. So he walked upstairs and wrapped on the door and pulled the excuse.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: What do you want? What do you want?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Selling fire extinguishers.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Out jumped the old man and said, where's the fire?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Show me the fire.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Where's the fire?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Show me the fire. Back and forth in the hall. And the old salesman, he went down the stairs. And he says, Price, that man up there is crazy. And the whisker come behind and laughed, and he said, I know how to get rid of those salesmen.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, he was the real character, that fella. And then when the Depression was on, I was sitting out there on the bench. And he used to come over to my station every day with something, you know. I was sitting there and he said, I'm looking for work. And I said, there's no work around here. The Depression is on and wheat is only worth about 30, 40 cents a bushel. There's no work here at all. So he took off. Old whiskey. You get up. That's the funniest man I ever seen in my life. He said, I'm 80 years old. He is looking for work and I've been trying to get away from it all my life.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, there was something in some truth in that, all right. There's always some work to do, you know. Yes, I'm 80 years old. I've been trying to get away from work all my life. He said, the Aspetta is looking for work.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, was this guy sort of a crazy guy when he was young too? Or just when he was old did he get so funny?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, I guess he was pretty far away. I didn't know him too well then because we lived far away. But he come home and bought a cow from us. He was a butcher, you know. He had to leave the cows so I had to go with him all the way. Walked nine miles and back. He took it up to his place in Butch Ridge. I was only about 70, 80 years old I guess.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, I remember that pretty well. About eight miles high, every 16 miles I had to walk back and forth. When I was a kid, of course, there was nothing I guess. I thought he gave me a quarter but I didn't get nothing.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He bought beer too, you know. He always come with a couple of bottles in his hand and walked up to his apartment. He would come up to me every day. Then he met the preacher.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The preacher said, You're getting too old for this stuff, is it?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yes, he said.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I've been figuring on buying you jokes from now on, he said.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, he had an answer for everything. He sounds like it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He was pretty clever but he come in and stole some money from up there. He told me one time in his room. They come in through the vent on the roof, you know, down to his room. But I know how to catch them. He said, I know if it's kids or if it's grown up people that's robbing me. So I took and put some fake money and real money in the drawer, he said. Then when they come, you can tell, he said, who it is. If it's kids, he'll take the fake money and everything. But the older people, they'll take the good money. He said, that's the way to find out.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That would narrow it down but it wouldn't tell them who was doing it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I'll see if I got any more stories. I don't know why. I'll put down some.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I told you about Oscar Berkeley and the Westerdolian guy. Huh -uh. Huh -uh.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, he was a wizard in figures, you know, by the other kings too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He could figure so fast. He told me he was down at the service station. He said, turn around and write down about fifteen figures on the paper, he said. And I'll show you how I can handle it. So I did that. Then I turned around and he went over and looked. That fast he had it added up.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So he called me in the service station one day.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He said, have you got a gun?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yes, I got a gun. Well, he said, I want to borrow that gun and then you take me out in the car. He said, I want to kill myself.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Why, I said, why?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He said, I haven't had anything to eat for three days, he said.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And why do you want to drive way out here?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Drive me out of the county, he said. Take me out of the county. He said, I don't want to die in the county, he said. So I said, oh, don't fool around like that. I'll take that for you. I knew the commissioner well, you know. He meant business though. He would have done it, you know. So I called up the county commissioner right away and told him the story. So he went out and seen him. Everything was all right. But he meant business. He could tell it on the way he acted. No, he said, I don't want to die in the county. I want you to take me over the county lines. And then I would shoot myself, he said. Get rid of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think the commissioner did for him? What do you think the commissioner did for him? Did he give him food?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah, you see they got food from the county when they were in hard shape. But he didn't know that, you see. He didn't know it. And he used snus, you know, and he had a box maybe for ten days. And it worked on him. He's getting up in years too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Boy, that sounded like a hard time. Then Alfred Westland, his name Mox Mox. Of course he looked so much like him. In Indian he was dark. He was a sweet tune. But he was a hell of a nice guy, that Mox Mox. He used to talk to him quite a bit, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, that was just his nickname because he looked like Mox Mox?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, his name was Westland.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He had top jobs in the sawmill too, you know. I worked with him there. He used to run the edger, you know. The one that pushed the lumber to, you know. And that was next to the sawyer. Paid. So he was transferred from the edger to the sawyer to give him the sawyer job. He come to me and said, I can't figure out this country or this world at all. Here now he said I've been having a job that's real hard work, see. Get three dollars a day I believe he got rich. And the sawyer got four. And here he gave me a better job, he said. And then more money.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Have you heard anything so crazy?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And he was no fool, that guy. He was smarter than that one, you know. I learned a whole lot from him alone. He was another bachelor, he was a bachelor too. My brother Bob didn't know that he had a cabin down here at the cabin. He had all kinds of history and stories, you know. So I used to go down there and listen to him. I learned a lot from those people. They were good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your brother and he were good friends?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, they told about the Roman Empire and all that stuff, you know. And the Roman Empire was no different from the United States, it is now.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You know that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, they didn't have machinery and cars and all that. People also didn't want to do nothing finally. All the big arenas were built up, it's copied from them, you know. And all the games they played is copied from them.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And they got so didn't want to do nothing and that empire stopped for a thousand years, you know. And here they come from the outside and just take some over like that. Because they didn't care to do nothing anymore, they just wanted to play. They had races, you know, and duels and everything in those places, you know. And they didn't care to work. So we might go that way, be careful. Then they said, take this book home with you old muck, and read it, you see. That was Mark Anthony's book I read.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Have you read that, maybe?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I started reading that book when I got about a quarter way through.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Am I reading about the United States?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I said to myself, it sounds like they had Republicans and Democrat parties and all that kind of stuff, you know, that we have here. They taxed business and they taxed the people so high, you know, they quit. That's why the Roman Empire went under. But they stood for a thousand years though. When you were reading that book way back then, you thought it was like the United States. Yeah, well that's about fifteen, twenty years ago, I guess, since I wrote it. I thought it was the United States, I was reading them out for a while, the way it sounded.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I think that's all I got. You tell me if you got any more stories written down there, just tell them to me. I think that's all I got. Two things. Two things of my life I can't figure out. I just can't figure them out. That happened in the Army, Mother. I never told the telegraphed much because nobody, there's no way to explain it, nobody breathes it anyway, so I haven't told anybody. There's nothing funny about it. When I was in the Army on the coast, it was a heavy fog. That's what I want to start with so you got something to go by, maybe.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I got up the next morning and fog was all golden, see? The sky was so clear and the sun was so bright that I've never seen anything like it in my life, you know, see?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Because it was down there where fog was all the time anyway. I felt the fog all good. I didn't believe myself. I had no, you know, people scratching their elbows, no effort, nothing like that. My head was clear and when I walked, there was no effort at all. No effort at all to walk. I said to myself, if people feel that way, then I'll be heated all my life. There's no fooling around. I told a few people, oh, you're talking drink of whiskey, he said. That's what made you feel it. I said, that's no feeling at all comparing to what I'm telling you. It's entirely different, I said. I don't want it to be a stroke, I said. That's nothing. You feel good and jump around. This was real, I said, real. And in a half an hour, it was golden. I was back to myself again. I walked across that. If people would feel that way in the world today, it would never be any trouble, any fights or a thing. It couldn't be. You couldn't be mean if you wanted to, the way I felt, you know. So I figured that all people don't feel that way. I just thought that because it wasn't much different, you know. That doesn't sound crazy to me at all. It sounds like you were just... Well, I think that nice weather had a lot of effect on me, too, and the sun being some beautiful hills coming over there. Just up over the ocean, you know. And that fog has took all that stuff out of the air, so the air was good, you see. See, I always had some pains all the time, all my life, you know. And none of them were there. That's what puzzled me. And when I started walking, I was just like I was floating alone. There was no effort. No effort. And I suppose what done then maybe was all your muscles were functioning together like they should do, you know. I suppose that's why I walked so easy. It's about half an hour. Actually, the same story. When I got back to the tent, it was four or five, I was sleeping in the tent, you know. And the other one that I read about now, so I think it's something to do that all right. In Spokane in 1918, it was,

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I brought me a car up there, an old Chevrolet touring car. So I went out, I was going to drive it, and I drove down in the railroad section. There was a lot of railroads, you know. When I got stuck on the railroad, the front wheels jumped over all right, but the back didn't.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You know what I'd done?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The train was coming. I jumped out of that car and tossed it off the railroad track, believe it or not.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: See, it wasn't so easy. That fellow I told you about, Anderson, that big guy, you know, he could have done it easy, but look at my arms.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: How did I do it?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You know. But I found out it's possible all right. I read two or four stories about it here. It's a blend in your system here somewhere. When you get scared or something, that thing functions. And you can run like a deer, and you can jump over a wall or anything from there. So I guess it works on you that way all right. I read in the magazine where the car wreck and the people was locked inside the doors, couldn't get it open, and everybody got so scared. You just tore that door off like it was paper. String, you know, that you had. So I guess something to that, but it's feeling good, though I can't, I can't quite figure that out, though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever had the feeling any other time except that time?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Never. Never.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It sounds like it was almost a religious experience is what it sounds like. Sometimes when people talk about religious experiences being a pure breakthrough, that's what it sounds like. But it wasn't religion, it was something else. It was just joy of living. Well, I think that the beautiful, some, you know, the air feelings were good, you know, where you breathe it and all that, had something to do with it. And the Southern was a perfect, you know, you never hear a reed or write something, you know, with smoke or something in the front of it all the time. But there, that big fog that we had, you know, it cleaned up everything. So everything was so dark all night, you know. It does come in a clear ting, though, I can never figure that out. We found them there, if we could see that all the time. There would be no trouble, no place.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So I guess we're a long ways yet from being perfect, huh?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Carl, would you explain to me again what you were talking to me about last time, some, when you were talking about a person and his environment?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Because this reminds me of that, some, you know, what a person does and how his environment affects him. Sure, that's something you can see yourself if you start thinking about it. It's there. If you live all by yourself, like a hermit, you know what that would be. You just sit there, wouldn't you, and eat, and sit and eat. Nothing happened to you at all. Of course, there's nothing there to shade you. But you meet a different person and he comes and tells you this, and does this, and what's happening around you. That's what you're living with and you're incorporated in that.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Because it, well you know what you had on television a lot of times, you tell where you're from, the way you talk. Which state you come from in the United States. You've got a different world, a different sound because that's the way people talk there. See, that's because you live there. That's why you have talk like that. Now the Southerners are over easy to figure out, you know, you can tell that yet, and not so much as it used to be. If you lived in a certain community, you'd have a different world, a different language, and a different way of acting and living. So people think you're responsible for everything, but I don't believe in that. It's the environment that does it. And the way you're born. That's what old Clarence Darrow did when he freed himself from buying out the uv. He said, I'm not responsible for myself. He said, I have nothing to say when I come to this world. I didn't put my own brains here. I didn't put my own shape on me. And I'm like a doctor. I'll do anything to save my client.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Didn't they confess right there?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And he went free and the jury was crying. And the judge, tears rolling out of his eyes too. It was a long speech he made before you were there. So I believed in him a whole lot.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You didn't have anything to say about that, did you? If you could change it, you'd be different maybe, wouldn't you? You'd be bigger or stronger or something like that, wouldn't you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: There you are. So you have nothing to say about that. And he said, I didn't put my brains here. I got nothing to say when I come. I got nothing to say when I leave.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He confessed that he bought the uv off when he said, I'll do anything that a doctor will do to save my client. Anything. So he paid the uv off. They claimed he bought the uv off once in his case. He never lost a case in his life. He was a criminal lawyer too. So you can see, it's many things. That's the way I look at the whole world myself. Do the best you can and that's all you can do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you telling me that it's good that Darryl bought the jury off? Are you trying to tell me that was good that he did that? Just that he couldn't help himself?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, he didn't mean that. He meant they hired me to protect him.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And you go to a doctor and you expect the doctor to cure you, don't you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So the doctor will do anything he can to save you in my young business. To save the clients. So I'll do anything to save it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So that meant that he bought the uv off. Feels like old Eisenhower, when he was campaigning, he said the same thing. I remember that speech so well. He said I'll do anything to win the battle.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: They asked him about some certain questions, how he's going to run the country and all. He said I'll do anything to win the battle. That's what Darryl done too. He bought the uv off to save that man. Of course he wasn't a crook, he was one of the best lawyers that ever lived, I guess, for that concern, for ordinary people, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what you were telling me, you started to tell me before about how you got into arguments with a couple of people about whether or not it was they that made things or whether it was the environment that made things happen?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah. The environment that, I just call it 90 % of what you do is the environment, the other 10 % you control, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Just like you, you don't have to eat, don't you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So you eat. And you see. And you can walk. That's what you're doing. But the other stuff, that's the environment that pushes you around.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That's the way I see it. He's only about 10 % of the person, he gets control and the rest he hasn't. He won't, if he's the diamond studder, everything, he won't, I won't do this. You can't tell me nothing, I won't do this.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And you tell him the same thing, you know, you won't get no worse than either, will you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: His wife and him. And I said that to him, but he believed in him right away when I started telling him. And he didn't, you know, where he lived. And he said, it wouldn't have been for Ed Soderstrom having a gray mare, he said, we'd never been here, you wouldn't have been here either today. That's how I got to quit. Took you out the first time, he said. See, I had one horse, he said, but we had a cutter and we had to have two horses. So we met, so the parents put on one horse and Soderstrom put the other one, went out and seen these girls, you know, and that's how he got acquainted. If I wouldn't have had that gray mare, he said, I maybe never would have knew you. So that horse done it, he said.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: See, well, that's easy to figure out, too. We just stopped taking. Just like I told you about Yasmin getting run over with the train, you know. He used to live in our house down there when he had a rock crusher here.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I got the news I happened to be up, the news at 11 o 'clock, I mean, that's a couple of years ago, and I'm born there three years. And he was hauling rock up around Portland, and the feller stopped him. He wanted to see about something, about a quarter mile from the railroad track, you see. And he stood there and talked a while, and then we got through and old Yasmin started up. I suppose he had some on his mind, too, what that feller wanted, and he didn't think about the train, I guess, and got killed right there on the railroad track.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But if that man never stopped him, he'd be in the old railroad track long ago, don't you see?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And that's what, that changed his things, you see. It didn't because anybody wanted to harm you or anything, but that's what we're living with.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: How do you happen to come up here?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd like to talk to you, or have you talk to me. You said Frank Brockey. That's true.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, if it had been for Frank Brockey, you wouldn't have been here, would you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, probably wouldn't. No, because things have been all changed, you see. See, I'm kind of a friend of Brockey, you know, and he knows I lived there all my life, and that's why he sent you up here. See, so there you are. You can prove everything, you know, if you stop and think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me what Bob was like, your brother, that taught you so much?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, he was a born genus, that's all, but he didn't use it. Something like Oscar Berkley, you know, he didn't use it. He was kind of queer that way. He figured that if you come and ask him something, then he'd tell it to you, but he figured you should know that, too. That's the way he was, see. He figured that you should know that because he knew it. Memory and all that. There was no name for it, you know. You could remember every damn thing. You could spell probably every word in the dictionary, but he never went to school much. He didn't go to school no more than I did, the last minute. But he was, he liked to read and study himself, you know, he built himself up from that. He took a short course from the university for three weeks to learn how to be an S -hearer, and that's all he spent there. So he was an S -hearer, too, you know, you could S -hear a rock, you could see it was gold or silver or anything. He had all the chemicals, and he died when he moved out of there. He had a whole case like this, full of bottles, all over. I was heading to my brother Albert, the one that lived in Moscow, I said, we don't dare to mix these up, let's take them to the dump yard because they might explode. And that's true, you know, he pulled two chemicals wrong. Bang, ignore, you know. No, you see that's what they used it for, if you come in with a piece of ore and there's some mineral sign in it, you'd use a chemical and then if it was a blue color, maybe it would be silver in there, or a brown color, it might be lead, or so on. He had all that, you know, so you could tell what kind of mineral it was, and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did he work as an assayer for very much of his life?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, he did not use for his own, he just took that course for his. Of course, he was doing so much prospecting all the time, him and Albert in Moscow, they would always open up the clay bed. Clay bed here, they opened that up, and it's named the Olsen Clay Bed now. The government sent people down here, so I was along and drilled holes, you know, with the government man when he was here. And Bob worked on it too. And then they had some mike up in the mountain there too. So he was a real, well, it was two guys I told you about before, that come down here before he died a couple of years. And they wanted to go out and look at some gold property they had. When they come back, you went into a vertical boom, and he had that lumberyard here that burned down, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And he said, what is that man doing in Troy?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I said, he said, I've never seen anything like it, they said. I've never seen anything like it in my life. He could name every damn rock he picked up, or any damn thing, he said. And that was true. But he never makes much for the people, you see, so he wasn't known very much for that reason. Not like I am, you see, if I had all that stuff for you, I'd spread it out to everybody.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, but that's the way you are. You're born that way, and you can't change yourself very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Bob spend a lot of his time by himself studying?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah, yeah, he studied everything. History, Bible, and all that kind of stuff. He could ask him for any questions about those things, and he knew them. He knew even the dates when it happened, or what, all this, you know. That was a clear part of it. In politics, 100 percent, you do everything about the United States, the Constitution, and all that, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: About the presidents that run, and how they got defeated, and all this, you know, all that stuff. Anything, you don't care what it was. He told me that he told you a lot of things that happened when you were too young or before you were born to remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are some of the things that you've been telling me that he told you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, I suppose I forgot everything. Well, I mean, there are, I figured that a lot of that stuff that you tell me. He told you. Yes, here, Osseberg, he told me all that, because he was big enough to remember him, you know, and I wasn't. You hear it, you tell about it, you know, and other people do, and other things like that too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So he remembered the things that had happened real well too. He had the dates and everything in his mind for a long time, everything, what year it happened and all that too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, he was pretty good.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, it's a bad human. I know you could have went to him and you would have got a whole lot more real happenings that he remembers, especially in mining.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He knew everything. I don't know how he was mining, but that was silly. Had an old shovel there. I don't know what make it was. And the clutch went out in it, see.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: We didn't know where to buy a clutch. He said, that's the same kind of clutch the case got in their tractor to go to Spokane and get one, and that's what it was. It was a whole, whole, well, it was a half -yard shovel, you know. Forgot the name of it now. It had no connection with that at all, but he knew that, you know, by looking at him or something. He run the shovel up there himself, and he could do anything, you know, without any experience. He never seen a steam shovel or a gas shovel before, but he went up there and run it like he had run one for ten years. That much difference, see. We had another one that run the shovel there. He ruined it before he learned how to, he dropped the dipper and knocked the teeth out of it. Instead of handling the brakes right, you know, dipper get away from it and fall down on the bedrock and bang. So we had to take it to Spokane to get it straightened up. But nothing like that happened when you run it, and you never run a shovel before. And the same with the steam engine here. He got a job from the schoolers in Genesee to run the steam engine when he was about 18 years old or something, 18, 19 I guess. And he had a smudge fire down there, and he had credit for the whole thing, saving. Well, he knew what to do. He never got excited.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: See when you got the smudge explosion, they had a wooden machine too, I believe. A lot of engineers when they got excited would shut the steam engine off right away.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You know what would have happened then?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, all the straw in the machine would have burnt up the machine instead of, he kept, he speeded up the engine. And he blown it out through the blower, see. So they saved the machine, and it damaged it somehow right. But you see if they left, if you wanted to push it out, well he never seen a spud explosion before. But he knew what to do. So he got a big credit for that, you know. Everybody talked about him saving that machine, they just couldn't get over it. Of course, he got so excited that he shut the engine down and run, I guess. Now, maybe he never done those things at all.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Carl, the IWWs, the Wobblies, got blamed for a lot of those explosions and fires in the fields around that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was much truth to that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No truth at all. Because they didn't know what smudge explosion were before because they never had so much smudge, see. And then they imagined they were running through matches in the bundles, you see. They come down to, when I had, when we had had the ultimate explosion down in Hope's Place there, you was down there the next day and wanted to find out if I had an IWWs working in here and all that.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And who are the fire stories?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Started, I said, it just exploded, that's all. We had one before, day before, I said, but that went out because it was different type of smudge. It was a black smudge, green -black, you know. And there was so much there that just choked fire off. But this red, the brown smudge, that was a real explosive, you know, and it wasn't too much of it, so it mixed just right with the air, you know. And it just went like that, you know, and the fire shot out all over you. And I said, that's what caused it. There was no IWWs or anything around here. All the people that were working for me had one. I know, know for the last ten years, farmer and farm boys, I said, all of them. No strangers at all. But they had that story going, you know. There wasn't nothing to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how come there were never smudge explosions before about that time? What do you mean by that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, they didn't have no smudge in the way, part of them. See, the smudge come in afterwards and diseased. See, and then when they got the blue petrol, the petrol would wait for them and they wouldn't get the smudge. But they got some, but not so much.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then they laid trash machines, you know. They made a whole lot of different things because they were speedier and they created electricity too, you know, for the belt somehow. A warm machine, certain days, you go back to the blower belt. There was a long one on the cylinder back to the blower, you know, and it went pretty fast. Certain days you could put your finger and see the sparks fly. See, and that's what started this. What explosions, you know, from the electricity. And then if you happened to get a piece of rock or something and fly in the cylinder, you know, it teeped. It would throw a spark too, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But the black smudge, that didn't fire off very good, but it went alright, but it woofs. And that's what fooled me. I didn't know it was so much, I also would have watched closer down there maybe. I looked at this sweet, and the bundle's always done that to see how much smudge there is so we can be on the guard. Well, it was a head here and there only. I said, that's nothing. Look at the smudge we had yesterday. Thirty percent. We had an explosion already, I said, but it went out itself. And here's just a few heads. It had set off though, because it was a red smudge. I didn't know anything was moving the red, and then I found that out afterwards. The red smudge was much more explosive. And then the mixture had to be right, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: When I was stretching there, I seen two or three machines burn up from our ridge. I could see across the other ridges, you know. I seen the smoke going up. There was one there, there. Oh, it was five or six machines went up, I think. Some smudge. There was an awful smudge there that day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that the story was going around, that they were trying to say that the Wobblies did it? Why do you think that was the climate?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, there's something like the communists going on today. It's about the same thing. Talking and talking about something you don't know anything about. The reason is... They were fighting the unions then, you know, the big outfits. And the idea of the W's, they were a union, you know. They wanted to make one union, you see. Together, not to have one brought on the Eagles or the whole works. And that's what they were fighting. They wanted to get rid of them, you know. So they brought up everything against them. They could find it. It was some tricky one. Soon you'll find that in any old thing, I guess, the forest. There was nothing dangerous about it. They were trying to scare the people.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: In those days, the working men had pretty tough, you know. And I worked out. I didn't work out very much because we had the trashing machine and we needed a little air so we didn't have to. You had to carry your own banking roll. Walk through your yard. Sleep in an old bunkhouse where the bunks were just up. One big stove sitting there and all the wet socks hanging around that stove with stonking over there from your sweat from the sweet. You had to sleep with all that. And that's what the W's wanted to clean up more than anything else, you know. So yeah, but now I think the unions have owned nuts anyway. They're too powerful now. They're hurting the country. Striking when they shouldn't strike. Because everything comes to a saturation point, you know. Well then, when they strike, they have to raise that thing. See, they can cause inflation worse than anyone else now. Because they got automation and those people that works there, you know, they can pay them and pay them and pay them because they got automation. Making so fast, see. But when we come to buy it, then we have to pay for it. And then we'll have a lot of unemployment too, you know, if they don't change that. You should change that as time goes on, you know. We used to work 60 hours a week and Ford got it down to 40. Now I think it should be down to 30. Give everybody a job. Instead of letting one union keep striking, you know, then that's why hand labor is higher today.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Because they're working under automation, they're strikers, and you can make things that are going faster in order to fix nothing, throw it away and buy a new one. And that's no good for the country because then we run out of material after a while, like we're running out of gas and all that now. See, we don't plan ahead enough.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Normal. And then they, ah, and they call them conservative.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And what's the other one now? Liberal?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Liberal, yeah. You're a liberal. I'm a conservative. I'm a liberal and you're a conservative too, if you're a common man. Conservative is nothing but if you're not a conservative, you're no good. But they want to throw that thing to the people, they don't know what they're talking about. If your house is getting time for paint and you paint it, you're conservative.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You're saving something, aren't you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And when you're progressive, you want to build up the country, that's fine and they'll need it. So they get, that stuff is, they're fooling the people all the time. We're both, a good man is both conservative and progressive. So he is. But here's what you change the name. A tight rod and, ah, what the hell, a name for that one, ah, and, ah, a waster you could call him. He's wasting everything for nothing. That's the names you have on those people, but, but the conservative. Everybody fears the conservative, he'll never get nowhere. He throws away stuff before it's half wore out and all that, you see.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He's a miser, you know, that's what you call it. A real miser, you don't do anything, you know, you just sit there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it's bad that some people have, ah, do you think that it's bad that some people have a great deal of wealth?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That's the worst thing that's happening in a ruling country. We'll be in a feudalism zone. We went away from that, you know, because a few people would bring everything down. Then it would spread to slavery, you know. No, I'll tell you why.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see what's happening in the country, don't you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: When I was a kid, when they had 160 acres or 200 acres of land or 300 or whatever it is, you could go anywhere. You never seen a no trespassing sign. You could go fishing anywhere. You were free. Now, these big farmers, they can hold things and there's no trespassing, so you're put in a cell. You have to live right here in Troy, you can't go nowhere.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That's what's happening, isn't it?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The worst thing in the world is when they let the people get thousands of acres of land. That should never let happen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't it true, Carl, that there were real rich people and real poor people 100 years ago, 150 years ago in our country?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Most of them were poor and the rich come in afterwards, you know, when they had the people. If you haven't got the people, your money is no good because you got to manufacture and sell. Do something and sell, you know. And that's where they are falling down. They class the people to unclass here or anywhere in the world, of course. Everybody is necessary. If you don't have no customers, you have no business. If you don't have no money that lays the ties under the railroad, you have no railroad.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: If you didn't have a man that climbs the poles, you wouldn't have no electricity or telephone, would you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That man is just as important as the other one. But they don't look at it that way. They classify him.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: What would a country be for all these rich people if they didn't have the poor people to buy from and deal with? They couldn't make it, could they?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I don't say it shouldn't be being factory.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I don't believe in that 100 % at all. I believe they should be controlled. So they can't do those things. Now you see what happens to the factories. It'll be for maybe a general motor left and maybe a general motor for his own.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It won't make it a difference who runs the thing if it's run right so the people get the benefits.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: We're traveling in that direction now. Just like this energy business is going to catch us now. Just think what a jam will be at the start of war. And that's what's carrying them to death now, you see. You don't get no oil from Arabia or in there. We were going to be out of oil. We couldn't put our farms in. We couldn't run our airships or anything. Going to sleep on the job.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: People don't realize that. We're the most vulnerable country in the world. Three or four atomic bombs were paralyzed. Absolutely paralyzed. We couldn't get our wheat in. We couldn't do nothing.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Knock out all the power plants, where are you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No electricity, no nothing. No gas because you couldn't pump your wells or nothing. There's only one nation in the world that can stand a war and that's China. Of course, they haven't advanced that far yet. They live on the land yet over there. So a bomb will drop and a bomb in China don't mean nothing. Of course, they live anyway. But they're coming up. I'm not against this stuff, but I've been talking how they let stuff run away from themselves, you know. Not hold it down.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, we could have the best government in the world here in the run, right?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Shouldn't have no depressions.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Why should we have a depression?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: When we're a self -supporting country, frankly 100%. Now we're getting short of oil. That isn't because we haven't got it. We got it. They don't dig it out. That shale down there in Utah, they're trying to get that gold 10, 15 years ago. No, no, they didn't want to touch it. Then they fought the pipeline up in Alaska. Here we sit.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You can't run an agent that way. You've got to follow up with everything. You can't let anything get behind.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I mean, it ain't good for the people out. You take me now at my age, when you're younger, you don't know what'll happen yet to you.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: What use was it for me to save any money when I worked for a dollar a day, two dollars a day, or something like that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Maybe I saved up 10 ,000 my whole lifetime.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: In one year I was wiped out the way things are going now, isn't it? See? There's no sense in anything like that, you know?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No sense at all. It's the big money game, man. It's just playing with the money. Making the living on the money. The stock market I think is the biggest homburg in the world.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: How can the stock walk 20 points in a day and then drop 20 next day?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, it's the stock they got in the factory, they did something.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Did the dividends change that quick?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The stock is worth whatever the dividend you get out of it. That's the price of it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But you just keep on playing with it up and down, up and down, up and down. Just like a gambling game, playing poker, that's all it amounts to. So some people are making money by doing that. Well, sure. Then they got these stools out to start the... I read about it here in the magazine 10 years ago. They got sellers out that start rumors, you know, that something is going to happen and the job goes down.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And they'll buy it up these and then they'll have another one that goes around telling us something big is going to happen there. Let me go up there and sell it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That's the way to work it. Because you can see yourself.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: If you got a farm out here, if it produces 20, 30 bushel of wheat per acre, that's all that farm is worth, no matter how you play with it. That's all it can produce.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And the same with the factory. You can only make so many automobiles and so many of this.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: How can they stop?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Maybe at the end of the year they could jump up a little bit if you had a good year.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And at the end of the year, it was a poor year, they might go down a little bit now. They go up and down every day. That's what I can't figure out. Every day they go up and down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will you tell me something about what it was like in the Depression here?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, we didn't get hurt much here because we didn't have no labor problems this week often. And there were a lot of small farmers. And to tell you the truth, we never knew there was a Depression until about 1931 here. We had it in 1929 back there. It took about two years to effect us here. Of course, there were farmers, you know, most of them. And then now, if you've got a job and if you can't work all year round, you're in bad shape. When I grew up, there was no such thing as an all year round job anywhere.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Bookkeeper might have one, but what did he get?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He wanted another $56 a month. He only made $600 a year. But these small sawmills, 30 or 40 days work. Harvest, 30 or 40 days. And hay, maybe 10 days. And a few small logging offies going up here. They hauled in enough logs in the winter so you could run the mill about 20 or 30 days in a year. And that's what we lived with. So it didn't effect us at all here. And then the people were more united than they are now, you see. Smaller farms, you know. They all had their fuel. The woods, out in the woods, you know. The Depression, every cabin in the mountain was somebody in. Every empty house was somebody in. The biggest sanctions we had here in Troy was Depression. We had 600 people in the Depression. There used to be about 450, 500. Come from all over the country, back east and everywhere. Because they could live here. Because if you went in an old shack up in a mountain, there was your fuel, the wood and everything for you. And then the farmers raised eggs here and the butter and everything was pretty cheap, you know. And they had butchered their own hog and then they'd sell a half a hog to somebody else or a quarter. It didn't cost much. So we didn't feel much here in the Depression. The only thing that had done any trouble at all was the banks closing up. But our bank didn't get broke even in Troy. But there they went under and Bovill and Elk River, those three went under. But here in Troy, heck, I built my service station right in the Depression days. 1929. I opened up in 30. It was up on the wall there when I was building the shop up on a step ladder. I'll never forget that. I said, you're joking. I said, look out, you might fall. You're up pretty high. The stock market fell today. I said, oh, heck, there's not going to be much worse tomorrow, he said. I'd rather fall today then. And I didn't know anything about the Depression. Never heard anything other than it was the same thing here. We were living on the ordinary wages and there couldn't be any less of it. I was farming all over and the old small sawmills were still here. So we worked on them, you know, only about a month or so in a year. And then on the harvest, the wheat wasn't worth anything, but we had to trash it anyway. So then a lot of places you work, you see, you got your board, you know, you wasn't out of anything. You saw a mill to pay you so much a day and you got your board. And when you went out trashing, you got your board there. So you got something to eat if you didn't get the money right away even, see. So you see, that's why the Depression didn't hit much here. But when you get into the bigger cities, they don't show up. But here, I used to come in my service station, but a dozen at a time, you know. Some of them started hollering, some of them got pinched, you know that. Some people that started up were there and owed money, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I said, what you hollering about, you fellas?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: This is the best time I ever had in my life. That was true. You just come in from the farm, you know. I was still single, had my bed upstairs at the service station. Went across the street and had a hamburger, sold maybe 50 gallons of gas in a day. I couldn't see any difference. I had to do the same thing one of the most good times. The only difference I've seen, I knew the bank was going to go broke, so I started putting away all the cash in the safe. So I had $600 in the safe when the bank closed up. So I was named the Second Bank of Troy. I know. Because of the people down in old Confederate, you got to check for some weight. They got to cover a lot of weight together, I believe, and they checked it back to the coast and they got checks from there. They went in the bank and they were just going to cash them. Can't cash them here because they had to go over to the service station. They said he got all the money in town. So when Vella come in, they said, $25 check or something. He didn't, he couldn't hardly believe it when I paid him $25 for that check.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And it helped me collect bills, too. I come in there and want a little money and then I'll take off so much.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I'll pay the cash to pay the bill and when the bank opened up, boy, the check was all right. So I come out of the head there.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, I didn't feel the depression at all. And you trusted people. And you trusted people. I'm looking at them.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, that was horrible. I helped them out a little bit. I had a chance, too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I told you about the guy that called Wood, you know, when they throw it off. Did I tell you that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me it again.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: There was a fella that lived in Helmer and he's all his wood down in Temasco. 16 inch wood for, he got two and a half a cord. And that's a long mall, you know. And he owed me three or four dollars for gas already and he come down and just put another gas up by the brickyard here. And he said, I want to get three gallons of gas sometimes so I can get Temasco out of my wood. I said, throw off the wood and I'll give you three gallons to go home where you belong. He said, you know you can't feed your family. Get two and a half a cord from Wood, hauling them a twenty, thirty, forty mile trip. Throwed up that much gas for the day.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then I said, if you get a flat tire or something, where are you then?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He said, you're in the right. You already owe me six, seven dollars. So he threw it off the wood and went back. And I told him, go to the county commissioners for help. That's all you can do. He had two, three kids to feed, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Another guy come in there and he had an old model T and it was raining and wetter like it is down above. I got a couple of three gallons of gas I got to get pot luck, he said. I ain't got no money. You can have my coat, he said. But I gave him the gas, I said. Here's your coat. I don't want you to freeze to death. He come back and paid it too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah. No, it wasn't bad hearing the depression. Well, some of them took some losses, all right.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, what happened in 31 and 32 when the depression started to hit? And what kind of difference, what made the difference, what made the depression start to hit here?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, it's just like this one we have now. We haven't seen too much. It's just starting to hit us real here now, the inflation. We're at six months or so. Well, where the people are, you know, that's where it shows up quickest, you know. In big cities you get too many poor people right away, you know, and all that. And when you're set up supporting like we were here, you know, you didn't notice it much. See, you raised your own food, so you wouldn't have to worry about that so much. You had chickens and you had cows, you know, made butter, made their own butter, you know, and had their own eggs and then had some to settle. So then Tom got that and it didn't cost much, you know. My folks, I think, sold eggs at the lowest six cents a dozen one time. See, but we had to sell them because we couldn't use them all up, so whatever you got was profit.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, when you think about these good times and bad times, what times stand out to you as being the really good times in the past and what times were the worst times?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, the best times we had here, according to my mother, she ought to know more than I did then. That was in 1916. That's when the war broke out in Europe, you know, and we wasn't in it yet. Then you could sell your stuff and get more. You got more for a dollar than you ever did, she said. The eggs and butter got a little better price and the laboring people started getting work because they were selling stuff, you know, to that war. But then after you got in the war, then you start getting inflation again. But then for two, three years there, you know, see, the war broke out in 1914 in Europe. And she told me that that's the best times we had in this land yet. There was more for your money than any time before because it was more equalized, you see. And that was true, all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the bad times? Do you think the Depression was the worst time around here? Or do you think there's been worse times than that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, in 1893, that's before my time, I think that was a bad Depression. That hurt them all here for this reason that it was a wet fall and they lost their crops, see, wheat rotten. And that's all they had to sell, you know. It was all right for feed, I guess. You can feed the hogs with it, that's all right. And I think that was the worst times it went through.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And I don't know what caused that Depression either. It was before my time, two years before I was born, you see. But it was hard on the farmers.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Except one, he knew how to sell his wheat. I told you about him, I guess. I put the stovepipe in the bag. No, you didn't tell me that. Next year, you know, the wheat was all right. So then he had this whole sprouted wheat left over. So when he put it, see they bought everything in sacks those days. There was no elevators, all sacks. So he put the stovepipe right in the center of the bag, see. And he put good wheat all around the pipe. And then he filled the stovepipe up with that poor wheat. And then he sold up the sacks, see. And then when they tested the wheat, you know, they got a little punch, a punch in the sack, and then the wheat runs out. Well, he never run the punch in that deep, you see. So he sold quite a bit, and then somebody told the warehouse man about that. Next time, you come to the U -Ramp in the middle of the bag.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He's in hell. I was hoping that was him and Kendrick. I forgot his name. I bet he got surprised when he seen that old punch go in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does anything stand out to you, Carl, as being the turning point? If there was one turning point in the history of this country, if there was one particular time that was the most important change in this country, what would you say it would be?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, yeah, I don't know. See, we didn't know everything then like you do now, you know. We had no telephones, no radios, and most people didn't even have a newspaper. So we didn't know much from the outside here at all, or what you heard. The first newspaper my dad then was, they spoke to you twice a week. You come twice a week, twice a week only, but that was good enough. But you didn't have any there for a while. No papers at all. Then it was no use. You lived nine, ten miles from town. You couldn't go down and get your paper every day. You went to town maybe once a month only, sometimes. Once a month, you bought some groceries. So people lived here in Troy didn't know what was going on, all right. More than the people in the country. But they didn't get to know too much either. They had the telegraphs, then the use. When I first started to vote here, then we went down to the depot. And that's where they got the votes from the telegraph. They had a big blackboard down there and their names on it. And then the telegraph would come in, how many votes this guy got and so on, on election day. So you see, people didn't get much news.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did the news spread? Did somebody read a newspaper and then tell somebody else?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, they'd tell somebody else then, you see. Told the way in, but they had no phones, so they could tell everybody quickly. You might be four or five miles away from here, you see. So the only place you could pick up any news is when you went downtown here. You get to go downtown, you know, but you didn't go to town maybe twice a month. Someone not that much even. So you didn't get much news.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to you when you were Justice of the Peace? Huh? What happened when you were Justice of the Peace?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, it didn't much happen. I didn't run, they just put my name down. And I got two votes and then the other fellow's name down, he got one. So you see, I went between two to one. And I didn't want it. I told him, and then the prosecutor in Moscow comes down, he said, you better have it, you got to have one down here. He said, I have got it, he said. I said, I don't know anything about that stuff. So I kept it anyway then. And I married a couple. And fined one guy $25 for setting fire, that's all they had. So that's all I had to do.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about as road commissioner? What about that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, I liked that pretty good. I wasn't going to go on for four years, I guess. I got beat next time. They didn't like to have farmers, they didn't want one commissioner on the town here. Because they figured they didn't get the right representative.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I didn't want it the first time anyway. But they appointed me, so I took it. Nobody wanted them out in the country, you know. That was something too, that, you know, live where the roads were. See, they had one commissioner over there, so he had one there and one there. He said he could watch the roads.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You once told me something about how during the Depression you saw how people were out of work and roads weren't being built.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that? Can you tell me something?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, that's in the early days. You meant when they had poll tax to work out. Everybody had a $2 poll tax, you know. I think it was $2. Then they could work it out on the road if they wanted to or a pit. That's the way you got the roads built, you know, helped to build.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So it takes a whole lot of difference a day, and it used to be all right. I don't see how they got the roads built at all. Out in this rough country, timber and brush and everything and stumps and rocks. Of course, they dodged a lot of that. The roads was long and crooked too then.
