But that did follow the lines too, so we had a lot of up hills too. There was no hazard then. It followed the line between the two farmers, you know. The road would be like this, up the hills. And then when it got muddy, you know, you couldn't hardly haul a thing on those roads. So you probably had to wait on the snow in the winter to do the hauling. So the winter was one of the best things that was for them, especially the loggers. Because you couldn't log on a wagon, you know, with haul orders. They tried that. It didn't work. They'd break the wheels down and dust steel. Couldn't take much of a load, you know. So that didn't work out. Sled was the only thing. So that counted. They wanted a good winter. And they had pretty good winters too, for that long. A whole lot of wood and ties and stuff like that and the grain and all that too, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the winter pretty rough on the cars to get around on the snowy roads? On the cars?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: They didn't have any cars then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This is before 1920 then?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, we had...when you had the cars, they started getting them in about 1915. Somewhere in there, I think. There's a few of them before that. But anyway, from 1915 up, we say, when the snow come, they put the cars in the garage, took the wheels off, never used them at all. Never fingered on taking them out in the snow. When I was in service, agent, when I first started in Dallas in 1929, and half the cars were blocked then, they didn't use them at all in the winter. Well, they didn't have no gravel roads even. All mud roads, you know. You couldn't get out in the snow and fight that. You'd get stuck right away. You'd have to pry them out. Rub the head with a stick and see how deep the hole was, see if you could make it, measure it. You had a lot of that on the model T's. You didn't have too much flour anyway. And you'd measure the mud hole with a stick. It was over six inches deep. Why, you better not try it. Hm.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your being on the Troy City Council? You were on that for a long time, weren't you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Twenty -four years. Twenty -four years.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, the employment made it first time.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And I stayed on and on and on. They wouldn't let me quit, so I didn't care. I didn't have no business. See, if you got business in this small town, you better stay off the council, you know. That just hurts your business to be on there. You know, you had your something and on and on. So I didn't want to. I was just about ready to quit the service station when I took it. So I was on then from thirty -eight on up in 1960 -something.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Then I had three more years to go, you know, too, but I quit because I wasn't feeling good. My hearing wasn't good, and I didn't want to quit. So I quit. But I still don't know. I've been on for forty -seven years. What are a few of the things that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh? What are the type of things that would happen in the council while you were on there that were interesting? Oh, we lived in town here?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah. Well, we did a lot of stuff. We put in the sewer system, the water system from the mountain. Paid Main Street. It was that much money than before. That's the main out. And then we got the fire trucks and set up. Good fire trucks in the station were at. And a new dump ground.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And I don't know. That's about all the main things, I guess.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That's when we bought a lot of timber up in the mountains. We made big money on that. It sold about half a million feet here in our long go for the new water system. From the timber we bought from when I was in. We sold timber for a hundred thousand dollars, I think, altogether. Get a whole section up there, a little more.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The water system was the main thing. Of course, we had government help on that.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: A lot of them thought up from it and think it wouldn't work. We got it through though. You know, that's the best system. Whereas if you've got enough water, you don't have the pumping expenses. The flow was down itself, you see. But now the town has been growing and all these washing machines that come in and sewer. There's enough water. We had a couple of small ones before too. But they got a big one up there, you know. Got about a hundred gallons a minute.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And that's my well. I tried to get it in there when I was on there and I couldn't make it. So that's my money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You worked for the well a long time?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, I worked for the well for several years because I figured the pipeline is getting old in my bus and then we'd be in over -patrons. So we need something there and then if the water is too little, we can just start to pump up. And that's fucked, you know. Then we went to raise the pond instead up there, you know. That wasn't any good. So then this new mayor, he called me up two, three times, you know. And I said, up there on that Johnson place is the place to drill. And there's where you'll find the water. Well, they drilled and they went up on the hill first and went down 250 feet and they got about 20 gallons, I think, there. And then he called me up again and I said, well, that's the place to go. Because my brother, he was a geologist and I learned lots from him, I said. And so they finally went down there, you know what happened. They got down 50 feet and from there we had an artesian well.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Put a drill down about 250 feet. The reason I do that because I, if you know something about rock confirmations, you know, like he did. When you get down to Granite, Granite is solid, there's no cracks and there's no water in Granite unless you find a fish or a fault, see. And I said, when you drill down and hit the Granite, you'll hit the water down there at the Granite and not in it. And that's what is up here, I said, I think. Because the mountain is sticking up there and the slope's down this way. And then the basalt has flowed in here. You drill through the basalt here, you hit the ground, you'll have water. That's what happened, but I didn't think it was going to be a well like that. What happened was a big sand streak in there and gravel. See, that's what the hell it is, like a dam up there, I guess. It's a big middle up above too, you know, and all that flat. And in the lower end, then the canyon comes together this way and that's what we drilled just above that. So it's a dam on the ground that we're getting the water from, just like the Felswelz I showed them to you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The what?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Therefore, I got a toothbrush. I had a cousin live there, he took me down to the wells. He wanted to go down and see the Felswelz.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The artesian wells.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: See the mountain range over here? There's a big basin in there and that's a pinch off here for me right here, see?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Here's the biggest well just flowing up. And it's the same thing up here in this mountain, only on a small scale. That covered hundreds of acres, thousands of acres in this basin.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then the water flows down to Ogden City now, you know, they don't have to pump it, see?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And that's one thing I use to compare it up here so much.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The same because you got a mountain back there and you got a stream coming down and the lower end is blocked up so it's built up a dam, see?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And that's what I've done here. See here's a narrow part of it, where that first well is.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Then you get up there and you're in a big flat, see?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But they're going down, they told me, you know, they're using them so much and you have to pump maybe after a while, but now they don't have to pump at all. It just flows into the pipe and down she goes.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Knowing then we had proof, you know, here. I told them then we had proof because the northern Pacific really well 500 feet deep here by the depot.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And I asked my brother, why didn't they get water in that well?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He said they started in the granite.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And we're in the granite all the time, see?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then we drilled one up on the ball diamond and they didn't get no water there. So I said there was no use to drilling this well because it's got no basin anywhere freely. Unless you go way up you might have had one alright. But they didn't get where they got one well here right in town that pumps about 15 gallons a minute. That's down only 60 feet. And that's down to the granite there then. That's where the water is there, 60 feet. If you go any deeper you hit granite and then you won't get no more water unless you happen to get the fault. It's broken up. So you get pretty all your water in the salt because that rock is slowed in, you know. It's full of pores, holes, cracks and basins, you know. It looks where it'll have has flowed, you know. Big caves even. And then if it's any block off anywhere then that water stays up there. It's like a dam who lives on the ground. And that's what happened here. And it poured out. But it surprised me that when it was that big well there, you know, the feller. That water commissioner, I hired him. He's still on here, you know. Couldn't spend all the time hiring a man that could run anything. So I told him, you've got to get a man that has had a business before so you know to plan ahead a little bit. So I said Durham is the guy to get and we finally got him and he's still here.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So he come and told me, he wished, he said, you've been trying to get a well?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well up there for long. So I got to look at it. He said his water is coming up everywhere. And then Durham believed him so I went up. And he was pumping it, you know, and he had a little river running. So I thought if he get a well, maybe 15, 20 gallons or 50 gallons or less, that he would pay to have it on a pipeline, you know. Because he'd give you the help and you know, they'd have to lay in the pipeline and nothing.
